coredata.dc:title: Activity detection of lactic acid bacteria based on fluorescence color difference of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Zhixin
Cao, Ming
Chen, Yongshi
Cheng, Long
Qin, Shuaifan
Tian, Bo
Jiang, Zhanmei
coredata.dc:description: Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) activity detection is crucial in fermentation production and quality testing of probiotic products. Fluorescence microscopy can be used to detect bacterial activity, but the fluorescent dyes commonly used are expensive, highly toxic, and have poor stability. This study synthesized boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and phenylboronic acid as raw materials, which had good fluorescence stability and a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 65 %. They were almost non-toxic and had abundant functional groups on their surface. BN-CDs exhibited concentration-dependent, with particle size increasing and emitted light exhibiting a redshift as concentration increased. When LAB were labeled with BN-CDs, dead and live LAB adsorbed different amounts of BN-CDs due to differences in their electrostatic adsorption capacity and membrane integrity. As the concentration of BN-CD was greater than 100 μg/mL, dead and live LAB cells exhibited different colors under fluorescence microscopy based on concentration-dependent effects, and the activity of LAB could be determined by the color of LAB cells. In addition, we also used BN-CDs to stain other types of cells, such as macrophages, onion cells, and yeast cells, and were able to determine cell activity through fluorescence color. It can be seen that labeling cells with BN-CDs is a promising new method for detecting the activity of cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
LAB activity
Color
Concentration dependence
Cell activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanisms of BN-CDs staining different active LAB
Luminescence properties of different BN-CDs concentrations
Analysis of different adsorption capacities of D-LAB and L-LAB to BN-CDs
Other types of cell imaging with BN-CDs and real sample detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: BN-CDs characterization. (a) TEM and particle size distribution. (b) UV–Vis spectrum, emission spectrum (λem = 545 nm) and excitation spectrum (λex = 510 nm). (c) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (460–560 nm). (d) FTIR spectrum. (e) XPS spectrum. (f) C1s spectrum (g) O1s spectrum. (h) N1s spectrum. (i) B1s spectrum.

BN-CDs fluorescence stability and toxicity. (a) Effect of UV irradiation. (b) Effect of storage in the dark. (c) Growth curves of LAB cultured in media containing different concentrations of BN-CDs. (d) Growth of LAB.
(a) Images of M-LAB with BN-CDs at different concentrations. (b) Images of LAB with BN-CDs (400 μg/mL) under UV, blue, and green excitation.
3D fluorescence spectra and TEM images of BN-CDs with different concentrations (a, d 24 μg/mL, b, e 240 μg/mL, c, f 2400 μg/mL).
Schematic diagram of coffee ring effect and D-LAB imaging with BN-CDs based on coffee ring effect.

(a) D-LAB and L-LAB mixed with BN-CDs. (b) Centrifuged D-LAB and L-LAB mixed with BN-CDs. (c) Zeta potential of L-LAB, D-LAB, and BN-CDs. (d) Standard curves of BN-CDs concentration versus fluorescence intensity.
Images of multiple types of cells stained with BN-CDs.
Results of plate count and BN-CDs detection method unit: log CFU/mL.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The same superscripts within each row indicate no significant differences (p > 0.05).

head.ce:title: Activity detection of lactic acid bacteria based on fluorescence color difference of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of food science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) activity detection is crucial in fermentation production and quality testing of probiotic products. Fluorescence microscopy can be used to detect bacterial activity, but the fluorescent dyes commonly used are expensive, highly toxic, and have poor stability. This study synthesized boron nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and phenylboronic acid as raw materials, which had good fluorescence stability and a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 65 %. They were almost non-toxic and had abundant functional groups on their surface. BN-CDs exhibited concentration-dependent, with particle size increasing and emitted light exhibiting a redshift as concentration increased. When LAB were labeled with BN-CDs, dead and live LAB adsorbed different amounts of BN-CDs due to differences in their electrostatic adsorption capacity and membrane integrity. As the concentration of BN-CD was greater than 100 μg/mL, dead and live LAB cells exhibited different colors under fluorescence microscopy based on concentration-dependent effects, and the activity of LAB could be determined by the color of LAB cells. In addition, we also used BN-CDs to stain other types of cells, such as macrophages, onion cells, and yeast cells, and were able to determine cell activity through fluorescence color. It can be seen that labeling cells with BN-CDs is a promising new method for detecting the activity of cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) are a kind of probiotic bacteria(Garbacz, 2022), which have many functions such as regulating intestinal flora, aiding digestion, and enhancing immunity (Hernández-Velázquez et al., 2024). The activity of LAB is an important guarantee for production efficiency and product quality(Yang et al., 2024), and it is necessary to detect the activity of LAB in real-time during fermentation (Lamont et al., 2017). Furthermore, LAB activity detection can be applied to screen excellent fermentation strains, judge the quality of fermentation agents or probiotics, and optimize fermentation production processes(Yang et al., 2024). Therefore, the activity of LAB is an important indicator in the fermentation of LAB and the quality of probiotics.

Nowadays, there are many microbial detection technologies available(Pengcheng et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2021). Cultivation method is the most classic microbial detection method with high reproducibility, but it is easily affected by cultivation conditions, time-consuming, and laborious(Cui et al., 2020). Gram staining method divides bacteria into Gram-negative bacteria and Gram-positive bacteria based on their cell wall structure and chemical composition, and is commonly used for microbial detection, but this method requires multiple washes and staining, control of washing and staining time, and cannot determine the viability of bacteria(Choi et al., 2024). Fluorescence microscopy method has high sensitivity and can specifically stain dead and live bacteria. For example, propidium iodide can bind to the damaged cell membrane and nucleus of dead bacteria, emitting red fluorescence at 488 nm to label dead bacteria, while fluorescein diacetate can bind to lipase in live bacteria to produce green fluorescence at 480 nm to label live bacteria(Fabbri & Elissondo, 2019). However, traditional fluorescent dyes have poor stability, high toxicity, high cost, and a significant impact on the detection substance(Song et al., 2017). Flow cytometry can detect the viability of bacteria by combining with fluorescent dyes, but its instrument is expensive and susceptible to interference from substances similar in size to bacteria(Walsh et al., 2023). PCR detection method can sensitively and accurately detect bacteria, but the operation procedure is complex and time-consuming(Váradi et al., 2017; Vidic et al., 2017). Immunoassay can quickly and selectively detect bacteria, but it is expensive and has poor sensitivity(Zhao et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2023). Therefore, it is very meaningful to find a convenient, inexpensive, accurate, and rapid method for detecting bacterial vitality.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a popular fluorescent nanomaterial with extensive applications in fields such as food, biology, medicine, and optics due to their excellent properties(Molaei, 2019; Yi et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2020). Compared with traditional fluorescent dyes, CDs have the advantages of easy synthesis, low cost, stable fluorescent properties, and low biotoxicity, making them a good alternative to traditional fluorescent dyes and widely used in bioimaging(Wang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022). Nong et al. synthesized four types of guanosine-derived CDs (G-CDs), which were unable to stain live microorganisms due to their electrostatic repulsion or low electrostatic attraction, but could selectively stain dead microorganisms. Moreover, G-CDs had good fluorescence stability and excitation dependence, and different colors of bacteria stained with G-CDs were observed at different excitation wavelengths(Nong et al., 2024). Fu et al. prepared phosphorus nitrogen co-doped CDs (PN-CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield (QY) and good fluorescence stability. The PN-CDs could be used for activity detection of bacteria based on different images of stained dead and live bacterial cells(Fu et al., 2023).

The performance of CDs is closely related to their synthesis methods, carbon sources, and doped heteroatoms(Ai et al., 2021; Prayugo et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2021). Among numerous synthesis methods, solvothermal synthesis of CDs has the advantages of convenient operation, simple instrument and equipment, and the ability to effectively adjust the surface structure of CDs and change their properties(Prayugo et al., 2023). Citric acid (CA), as a high-quality carbon source, can provide abundant carboxyl groups and form stable carbon nuclei(Wysocka et al., 2024). Ethylenediamine (EDA) is often used to provide a nitrogen source, with abundant amino groups that can combine with carboxyl groups to form amide groups, enhancing the fluorescence intensity of CDs and increasing QY(Fu et al., 2023). Boron element is similar to carbon element in the periodic table, and their atomic radii are also similar, so it can be well doped into CDs(Fu et al., 2024; Yuan et al., 2020). In addition, when boron and nitrogen atoms are co-doped, different atoms can interact with each other and change their arrangement order, thus more effectively improving the performance of CDs(Carneiro et al., 2022; Kim et al., 2018).

This study developed a low-cost, efficient, and storage-resistant probe to meet the demand for detecting LAB activity. We used CA as a carbon source, added EDA and phenylboronic acid (PBA) to prepare boron nitrogen co-doped CDs (BN-CDs), and characterized them to understand their structure and properties. We stained LAB with BN-CDs, focused on the imaging mechanism of BN-CDs for detecting LAB activity, and also used BN-CDs to detect the activity of other types of cells, aiming to provide a novel method for assessing cell activity.

CA, EDA, and ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). PBA was purchased from Shanghai Shaoyuan Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). De Man Rogosa Sharpe (MRS), Yeast extract peptone dextrose (YPD) medium, lysogeny broth (LB) medium, and dialysis bags (500 Da) were purchased from Beijing Solabo Biological Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was purchased from Beijing Labgic Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Lactobacillus helveticus R0052 and yeast ATCC9763 were purchased from Shanghai Bioresource Collection Center (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli ATCC25922 and Staphylococcus aureus CMCC(B)26003 were purchased from Shanghai Luwei Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Macrophage RAW 264.7 and RPMI-1640 medium were purchased from Beijing Boao Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Onions were purchased from a local supermarket (Harbin, China).

The activated LAB were incubated in MRS medium for 24 h, centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min, and then the medium was removed to obtain bacterial sludge. The bacterial sludge was resuspended in PBS to obtain live LAB (L-LAB). A portion of L-LAB were heated at 100 °C for 15 min until all cells died, resulting in dead LAB (D-LAB). Equal amounts of L-LAB and D-LAB were mixed to prepare a mixed LAB solution (M-LAB) with half dead and half live LAB. The preparation of yeast, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus samples was similar to that of LAB. Macrophage was cultured to the growth balance phase and then resuspended in PBS. Fresh onion epidermal was stripped to prepare live plant cell samples, while dead plant cell samples were prepared by heating stripped onion epidermal in distilled water at 100 °C for 15 min.

BN-CDs were prepared by solvothermal method. 3.3 g of CA, 1.2 mL of EDA, and 0.05 g of PBA were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and then 10 mL of ethanol was added. The mixed solution was transferred to a PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the impurities were removed by centrifugation at 1000 r/min for 10 min, then the supernatant was taken and added to a 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h of dialysis, followed by lyophilizing for later use.

Fluorescence spectrum was obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF6000, Shimadzu Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). Ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectrum was recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). The shape and size of BN-CDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (2100F, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed through an X-ray diffractometer (EscaLab250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Shanghai, China). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded using a FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA).

The fluorescence QYs of BN-CDs were measured with quinine sulfate (QY = 55 %) as a reference(Ren et al., 2020), and calculated using the following formula:
(1)
Q Y =
Q R
×
(
I /
I R
)
(
A R
/ A
)
(
n /
n R
)
2
Where I is the integral area of the emission peak intensity corresponding to the excitation wavelength, n is the solvent refractive index, A is the absorbance, and the subscript R is the reference substance.

BN-CDs were exposed to ultraviolet light for 120 min and their fluorescence spectra were recorded every 20 min using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. BN-CDs were stored in the dark for 30 d, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded on days 0, 15, and 30, respectively.

LAB were incubated in MRS medium containing different concentrations of BN-CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 24 h. The OD value (600 nm) of the medium was measured every 5 h using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and then the LAB growth curve was plotted. 100 μL of stationary-phase LAB solution containing different concentrations of BN-CDs was evenly spread on a plate. The growth status of LAB was observed after incubating at 37 °C for 48 h.
Cell samples were stained with BN-CDs for 1 min, dried on a glass slide, and then observed under a fluorescence microscope (Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

The morphology of BN-CDs was characterized using TEM (Fig. 1 a). BN-CDs (24000 μg/mL) were well dispersed and the particle size was concentrated between 5 and 9 nm, with an average size of about 6.9 nm. The UV spectrum of BN-CDs (Fig. 1b) showed that the absorption peak at 200–270 nm was the π = π∗ transition of benzene ring(Li et al., 2018), while the peak at 345 nm was caused by the C=O group (Ma et al., 2020). In addition, the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum (Fig. 1b) indicated that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for BN-CDs with a concentration of 400 μg/ml were 510 nm and 545 nm, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectra of BN-CDs (Fig. 1c) showed excitation dependence, with their emission peaks exhibiting a red shift as the excitation wavelength increased. FTIR analysis of the surface functional group of BN-CDs (Fig. 1d) revealed that absorption peaks of O-H/-NH2 and -CH2 groups were located at 3280 nm and 2925 nm, respectively. The absorption peaks of C=O and C=C groups were at 1678 nm and 1550 nm, which may be attributed to the abundant carboxyl groups of CA and the benzene ring of PBA (Li et al., 2020; Simões et al., 2020). The absorption peaks at 1382 nm and 1044 nm were caused by B-O and B-C groups, confirming the successful doping of boron element in BN-CDs(Ngo et al., 2019). In XPS spectra (Fig. 1e), predominant peaks at 284.8 eV, 531.21 eV, 399.8 eV, and 191.81 eV were characteristic peaks of C, O, N, and B, respectively, and the atom ratios of the elements were 69.59 %, 17.07 %, 12.95 %, and 0.38 %. In C1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), the fitted peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.76 eV, and 288.23 eV corresponded to C=C, C-O, and C=O(Liu et al., 2019), respectively. The fitted peaks at 531.44 eV and 533.33 eV in O1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) were caused by C=O and C-O(Liang et al., 2024), respectively. In N1s spectrum (Fig. 1h), the peak near 400 eV was -NH2, where 400.1 eV and 402.24 eV were fitted peaks for N-H and C-N(Tang et al., 2024). B1s spectrum (Fig. 1i) showed two fitted peaks at 192.3 eV and 196.9 eV, which could be assigned to B-O and B-C(Chen et al., 2024). In addition, the fluorescence QY of BN-CDs was calculated to be 65 %.

In practical testing, the stability of fluorescent substances is crucial. As shown in Fig. 2 a, after 120 min of continuous ultraviolet irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs still retained 90.9 %. After being stored in the dark for 30 d (Fig. 2b), the fluorescence intensity was 65.9 % of the initial intensity. The results indicated that BN-CDs had great photo-stability, could be stored for a long time, and were suitable for real-time detection of LAB.

The growth curves of LAB cultured in medium containing different concentrations of BN-CDs (Fig. 2c) showed that even in a medium with a BN-CDs concentration of 800 μg/mL, the growth of LAB was not affected. The plate count (Fig. 2d) also showed that BN-CDs did not affect the growth of LAB. Both results indicated that BN-CDs had almost no toxicity to LAB, so when using BN-CDs for LAB activity detection, there would be no impact on the detection results due to BN-CDs affecting LAB activity.

M-LAB were labeled with BN-CDs of different concentrations, and the imaging results were shown in Fig. 3 a. M-LAB exhibited two colors of emitted light, and as the concentration of BN-CDs decreased, the colors of the two emitted lights gradually approached each other. When the concentration of BN-CDs was between 800 and 8000 μg/mL, M-LAB displayed two significantly different colors, but the high concentration of BN-CDs also led to a significant background effect. When the concentration of BN-CDs was in the range of 10–100 μg/mL, the color difference was not significant. When the concentration of BN-CDs was between 200 and 400 μg/mL, there was a significant difference in the two colors of LAB, and the background effect was minimal. Therefore, the optimal concentration of BN-CDs for labeling LAB was between 200 and 400 μg/mL. The optimal imaging concentration of BN-CDs for other cells was adjusted based on the number and size of cells.

LAB were stained with BN-CDs at a concentration of 400 μg/mL and imaged under a fluorescence microscope, as shown in Fig. 3b. Under different excitation lights, LAB with different activities had different colors. Under UV excitation (350 nm), L-LAB and D-LAB exhibited blue and green colors, respectively; under blue light (480 nm) excitation, the two appeared green and yellow, respectively; while under green light (530 nm) excitation, L-LAB and D-LAB were deep red and bright red, respectively. The activity of LAB could be determined by the color of the emitted light. Among them, dead cells and live cells had the largest color difference and the best detection effect under UV excitation. Yu et al. prepared sulfur doped CDs (S-CDs) which could rapidly stain dead cells through passive diffusion, while live cells had a complete surface structure that prevented S-CDs from entering, allowing S-CDs to only stain dead cells (Yu et al., 2022). Fu et al. prepared phosphorus nitrogen co-doped CDs (PN-CDs) that could stain the entire D-LAB and the cell wall of L-LAB, causing the D-LAB cell to emit light as a whole while the live only emitted light in the outer ring, but with the same color (Fu et al., 2023). Compared with the aforementioned methods, BN-CDs staining can simultaneously illuminate the whole dead and live cells, enabling clearer and more direct determination of cell activity through different fluorescent colors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of BN-CDs staining different active LAB
Luminescence properties of different BN-CDs concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: 3D fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs (Fig. 4 a–c) showed that as the concentration of BN-CDs gradually increased from 24 μg/mL to 2400 μg/mL, the fluorescence center also shifted red. Within a certain range of excitation, the higher the concentration of BN-CDs, the larger the emission wavelength, indicating concentration dependence. The TEM images (Fig. 4d–f) showed that as the concentration of BN-CDs increased from 24 μg/mL to 2400 μg/mL, the average particle size also increased from 0.5 nm to 2.3 nm. This may be caused by the aggregation of BN-CDs(Sadhanala & Nanda, 2016). Similarly, Wang et al. synthesized nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) by microwave pyrolysis of CA and ammonia. N-CDs exhibited concentration dependence, and their emission wavelength shifted red with increasing concentration. They measured FTIR spectra, XPS spectra, and particle size of N-CDs at different concentrations and found that the surface groups of N-CDs did not change with increasing concentration. Therefore, they concluded that at low concentrations the spacing between N-CDs particles was large and the inter-particle interactions were weak, leading to a blue shift of the emission wavelength. At high concentrations, the spacing between N-CDs particles decreased, the interaction between particles increased, and self-absorption occurred, resulting in a red shifted in the emission wavelength(Wang et al., 2018). Ba et al. prepared concentration-dependent CDs via hydrothermal method used CA and ammonium hydroxide, and the CDs could shift the emitted light from blue to green by increasing the concentration. They found that CDs with abundant surface groups were more conducive to aggregation with increasing concentration. Due to different assembly orientations and particle quantities, self-assembled spheres of different sizes were formed, resulting in wavelength shifted in emission(Ba et al., 2020).

BN-CDs (50 μg/mL) and D-LAB were mixed, and then 10 μL of the mixture was dropped onto a glass slide. Based on the coffee ring effect, BN-CDs concentration at the edges of the sample was much higher than that at the center after drying. As shown in Fig. 5 , as the concentration of BN-CDs increased from the center to the edge, under UV excitation, the color of D-LAB cells gradually changed from blue to green, and the emitted light shifted red. It could be seen that the difference in D-LAB cell color was mainly caused by the difference in BN-CDs concentration.
Therefore, we speculated that the different adsorption capacities of L-LAB and D-LAB for BN-CDs resulted in them displaying two different colors at the same excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of different adsorption capacities of D-LAB and L-LAB to BN-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The same volume of L-LAB and D-LAB were taken into centrifuge tubes, and then equal amounts of BN-CDs were added to each tube. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the D-LAB and L-LAB samples had similar colors. The samples were centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min to precipitate all bacterial cells. As shown in Fig. 6b, the sediment color of D-LAB sample was darker than that of L-LAB sample, while the supernatant of D-LAB sample was lighter in color compared to L-LAB sample. The color difference of the sediment and supernatant between D-LAB and L-LAB samples confirmed that D-LAB could indeed adsorb more BN-CDs compared to L-LAB.

As shown in Fig. 6c, the potential of BN-CDs was −6.9 mV, and the potentials of D-LAB and L-LAB were −15.2 mV and −31.5 mV, respectively. After adding BN-CDs, the potentials of D-LAB and L-LAB were −21.7 mV and −35.1 mV, respectively. Obviously, D-LAB adsorbed more BN-CDs leading to a greater potential change after adding BN-CDs. BN-CDs, D-LAB, and L-LAB were all negatively charged, and the electrostatic interaction between BN-CDs and LAB was mutually exclusive. However, L-LAB had a stronger repulsion against BN-CDs due to their larger absolute negative potential. In addition, various functional groups on the surface of BN-CDs could bind to the cell wall and intracellular substances of LAB. L-LAB had intact cell membranes, which prevented BN-CDs from entering the cell in a short period of time. While the cell membrane of D-LAB was disrupted, increasing its permeability, and making it easier for BN-CDs to enter the cell. Therefore, the amounts of BN-CDs adsorbed by D-LAB should be greater than that of L-LAB.

Furthermore, to determine the adsorption capacity of L-LAB and D-LAB for BN-CDs, we plotted standard curves of BN-CDs concentration versus fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 6d, there were two curves:
(2)
F
L 1
= 278.71 + 830.87 C
C ≤ 4.36
μ g / mL
R 2
= 0.97
(3)
F
L 2
= 4264.46
exp
− C / 110.51
+ 23.25
C
> 4.36
μ g / mL
R 2
= 0.99

Next, an equal amount of BN-CDs was added to an equal volume of L-LAB and D-LAB solutions with an OD value of 1 at 600 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity was measured. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was measured after the complete precipitation of LAB in the mixed solution. The amount of BN-CDs adsorbed by L-LAB and D-LAB was calculated using formula (3), and it was found that 1 mL of L-LAB and D-LAB solution could absorb 10.88 μg and 16.13 μg of BN-CDs, respectively. D-LAB could absorb more BN-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Other types of cell imaging with BN-CDs and real sample detection
ce:section.ce:para: BN-CDs were used to stain other types of cells. As shown in Fig. 7 , BN-CDs could label macrophages, onion cells, yeast cells, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus. Similar to LAB, cells with different activities exhibited different fluorescence colors.

In addition, LAB activity in fermented milk of different fermentation schedules was detected using BN-CDs detection method, and plate count was used as a control. As shown in Table 1 , there was no significant difference (P > 0.05) in the number of L-LAB between the BN-CDs detection method and plate count, indicating that the BN-CDs detection method had high accuracy and could be applied to real sample detection.

BN-CDs detection method has many advantages. Compared with the cultivation methods of microorganisms and the clone formation assay of cells, the BN-CDs detection method is easy to operate, do not require time to wait for cell growth, can detect cells that were not capable of dividing and can also stain and count dead and live cells simultaneously. Compared with other dyes in staining methods, BN-CDs have high universality and can detect the activity of multiple types of cells. BN-CDs are easy to prepare, cost-effective, and have stable fluorescence properties and high sensitivity. Importantly, they can stain both dead and live cells simultaneously without the need for multiple dyes. In addition, BN-CDs are almost non-toxic, so they will not affect the experimental results due to toxicity and are safer for operators.

This study prepared BN-CDs using citric acid, ethylenediamine, and phenylboronic acid as raw materials. BN-CDs had stable fluorescent properties, high QYs, almost nontoxicity, and abundant functional groups on the surface. Importantly, they exhibited concentration-dependent, with particle size increasing and emitted light exhibiting a redshift as concentration increased. LAB labeled with BN-CDs could exhibit different colors at the same excitation wavelength, mainly due to the difference in electrostatic adsorption capacity of D-LAB and L-LAB cells towards BN-CDs, as well as the difference in cell permeability caused by the destruction of D-LAB cell membranes, resulting in different amounts of BN-CDs adsorbed by LAB cells. In addition, BN-CDs could stain macrophages, onion cells, yeast cells, Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus, and were able to determine cell activity through fluorescence color. Labeling cells with BN-CDs is a promising new method for detecting the activity of cells.

Zhixin Xie: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ming Cao: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yongshi Chen: Visualization, Validation. Long Cheng: Software, Methodology. Shuaifan Qin: Methodology, Data curation. Bo Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhanmei Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2019C020).

Sci. BULL.

Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Liu, Luminescent carbon dots with concentration-dependent emission in solution and yellow emission in solid state, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 565 (2020) 77-85. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.01.007.
